speaker: interviewer
text: --a story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Say what the tape is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, this is Tape 2, with Leon Leyson. We're continuing.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about Narewka.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I will, as soon as I tell you something about when I used to hang around my father's place of work and watch him-- watch him do the work, and some other people who were working in the same area. So in my spare time, I used to hang around. And occasionally, they would allow me to-- to help in that. They had various pieces of equipment there. And one of them was a forge. In addition to the machinery they had, they had this forge. And some of that--

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know what a forge is.

speaker: interviewee
text: You don't know what a forge is?

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's where you heat steel in order to hammer it into shape.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so this was an old-fashioned kind of forge, except that it didn't have one of those bellows. It had a fan that you cranked, to make the-- make the coals really hot, hot enough to-- to heat the steel and be forging temperature. So they would let me crank that-- that handle. And I recall one incident, where they had-- one of the men was heating something. And I got to crank the handle. And I cranked the handle. And he had to go somewhere else and do something. And so I was watching that steel he had in there. And apparently, it was getting a little bit too hot, and so I slowed things down. And he didn't show up and I kept slowing things down. And then finally, when he came back, I heated it up a little more and so it was ready. And I recall with great pride that he told my father later that that was really remarkable, because if he had his regular helper doing that, this steel would have been burned long before, [LAUGHS] because he had taken so much time. And if I had really left the-- the temperature as high as it was originally, it would really have burned.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you know how to do this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I just lucked out, I guess. But I can remember that, hearing him tell my father about this remarkable little boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father, what did he say?

speaker: interviewee
text: He just shook his head and he knew. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he proud of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he tell he was proud of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He used to. He used to-- not in so many words, but he would let me do things that showed he was proud to see me do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you the youngest of the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am the youngest, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever feel a-- a different kind of relationship that he might have had for you from the other children? Did he play favorites?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. If anybody was a favorite, it was my sister. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Not the baby of the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not the baby. No, the sister was. She was the favorite.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me about Narewka.

speaker: interviewee
text: We left Narewka in 1938, and joined my father and started establishing ourselves. And-- and of course, the war broke out in 1939. And we had-- he had lost his job. And we started to have to go out and-- and help the family survive. Narewka was, at the time, occupied by the Soviets. When Germany invaded Western Poland, Eastern Poland was occupied by the Soviets, and so that included Narewka. At the time, we thought that we had made a big mistake in ending up on the German side, and that there was more-- a better-- the situation was better in-- on the Soviet side, at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in any case, as it turned out, in 1941, when the Soviets eventually invaded-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when the Germans invaded the Soviet Union, they went through that part of Poland and reclaimed that, claimed that area, as quickly as the tanks would carry them. And when they came into Narewka, behind the invading army came a special army, whose job it was to seek out Jews and murder them. So in 1941, on a day in August, they gathered all the Jewish-- the whole Jewish population. 1,000 people?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1,000 people. Separated the men from the women, drove the men to the edge of the forest. And the forest was not very far from us. It was a beautiful forest in-- near Narewka. And they murdered every man and child in-- near the forest, buried them in a common grave. The women were then kept in a barn for about three or four days, without food. And they were taken away and they were murdered at another location.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as a result, in that one day, every Jewish human being in Narewka died, was murdered, which included my grandparents from both sides, and my uncles and aunts. Everyone related to us lived in Narewka. And they disappeared in just this one day.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, at the time we didn't know this. We were-- 1941, by that time, we were already in a ghetto. And the laws against the Jews had been more and more restrictive. And so we didn't know this until the end of the war, in 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you come to find this out?

speaker: interviewee
text: We found that out because there were some people who had these connections with Narewka, who worked in this glass factory. And so when we came back after the war to Poland, my father reconnected with some of his old friends he used to work with. And they related this incident to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know who dug that grave in the forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: We visited that grave, in-- in Poland, in Narewka.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year did you visit?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1989, I took my family to Poland to visit. And I took them through all the areas where I was. And we also, of course, visited the common grave. The-- the grave was-- had to be dug by the people who were driven down the street, through-- to the-- to the edge of the forest and brutally murdered.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not one Jewish human being survived that particular action.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you hold the people of your city, the non-Jews, responsible in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they have a certain amount of guilt, in the-- in the fact that they're not guilty for the action itself, but for the fact that no one, not one person, had actually survived, has probably something to do with the complicity of the population in Narewka, the non-Jewish population in Narewka at the time. I'm pretty certain of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your family's possessions, their homes?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were just simply taken over by the non-Jews. And they proceeded to live in those homes.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the war, did you think of going back, perhaps to take over the-- to take back your family's houses?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I personally never did. I would never do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why not?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I don't-- I would consider that demeaning, to have to go back there and reclaim that home. I-- I think that. It's [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] done. You know, it's one of those things that's finished. And I would never-- I would never want to possess that home again, or my grandparents' house.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to proceed now towards back to Krakow, where you were a child--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, well--

speaker: interviewer
text: --and the war began.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the war began. And restrictive laws began against the Jews with these-- my-- my father lost his job immediately. He was working in this factory as a tool and die maker and he no longer could work there, because Jews could not work in factories that were non-war-related. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and Jewish ownership was prohibited and businesses were taken away from Jews immediately.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had told me that you all were responsible to help out. In what way did you, as a child-- how old were you then? This was 1941?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: 1939. You were how old?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was-- I was going to turn 10 years old that year.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would a 10-year-old child possibly help out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the area was-- where I lived was industrial, in that the glass factory was there and there were several other factories. And as a matter of fact, my father used to work at different factories because not every factory had a tool and die maker. So when they had work of that nature, they would ask him to do this for them. And there was a-- do you want to know about my first job?

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] There was a factory that made soft drinks. And so I went there to--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of it? It wasn't Coca-Cola.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gosh, I don't remember what the name-- I-- I don't know if it had a special name or the bottles were named all these different flavors.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of flavors did you make?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there was citrus or orange flavor, and some other kind of fruit flavor. In any case--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got-- I got a job working in this-- in this factory. And I thought that was great, because I could drink all the soda I wanted. But after the first day, I didn't care for much more soda. [LAUGHS] But I did get-- I did get paid for putting labels on the bottle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get paid well? What did you get paid?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, a small amount of money. I was able to take a bottle of the soda home, which was a nice treat for the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about the money? What did you do with that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just brought it home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was done with that money?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother would use it to feed the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the other brothers and sisters also working?

speaker: interviewee
text: They all did-- all did something-- something to help-- to help out, different places.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other children working in that factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was there, for-- for a short time, also doing the same-- similar thing, putting-- putting labels on bottles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the older people who worked at the factory-- were they aware of the fact that you were a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way did you identify yourself as a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, for one thing, the company was a Jewish-owned company.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who owned it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall the name of the owner any longer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- was it necessary at that time in Krakow, to wear a Jewish star, a yellow star?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, soon after the Germans came, the-- the Jewish star was required. And in Krakow, we didn't wear a yellow star sewed on. We had armbands. The armbands were-- you know, when they first came out, everyone had to wear an armband. We thought it was pretty silly. And I was-- I was a little boy. I never wore one.

speaker: interviewer
text: What color was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was actually white with a blue star on it. And the grownups were wearing them. And it was taken as, well, the war isn't going to go very much longer. And so if we Jews have to wear armbands, so that's OK. What difference did that make?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you get this armband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they were-- they were on-- for sale. You know, it was just one of those--

speaker: interviewer
text: For sale?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, for sale. You can buy-- you can buy an armband. You can even buy-- if you wanted to, you could even buy one that's made out of-- was made out of some kind of plastic, with a elastic on it or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what it cost?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't. But it's--

speaker: interviewer
text: And who sold them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Entrepreneurs. Jewish entrepreneurs, who made the-- made up the bands, the armbands.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose rule was that, that--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans. It was the-- the-- the governing German [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] committee, that determined that Jews would wear armbands and the Jews could not use public transportation. And that was one of the reasons for having the armband, so you could be identified.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about in your family? Did you all wear an armband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not me. But the-- my older brothers-- my older brothers wore one. And my father wore an armband.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your mother? Did she?

speaker: interviewee
text: She wore one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the house, did you wear it as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was-- as I said, when I-- as I-- in my recollection, it was sort of-- as I think of it back now, it was-- it was not such a bad thing. It wasn't the worst thing that happened. We  thought at the time it was the worst thing, but it really wasn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: What--

speaker: interviewee
text: You saw where they had little signs on the door, on the way out. They said, don't forget your armband. People had no-- couldn't remember they had to put an armband to go outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next? What happened next in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Things-- things got-- of course, getting worse, with the more restrictive laws against the Jews. And the ghetto was then started.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that? What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was in the end of 1940. And the Germans came in 1939. So by 1940, we were in a ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: This ghetto was actually in the-- on the same side of the Vistula River that we lived, in Podgorze, not-- not very far from our original place where we resided.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who told you have to leave your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was an edict. It was plastered all over the walls, in the kiosks. And everywhere you could imagine, there were these notices.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we went along with everybody else, just-- we received this-- this place, this housing. And huge numbers of people were just jammed into the area. And the area of the ghetto was surrounded by brick walls.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you-- where did your family end up living?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in a-- in an apartment house, with many apartments. It was several stories high. And we had one of the apartments. And then, of course, when more people moved in, we took in another couple, to share the apartment with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you take with you? Sorry. I didn't mean to speak over your voice. What did you take with you into the ghetto? What did you, as a kid, take with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't have any particular things that I-- that I-- that were exactly mine. But we just took our stuff, our possessions, our furniture, our beds, and things that people own, and loaded it up in one of those wagons, horse-drawn wagons, and hauled it off into the-- into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go willingly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not willingly, but there was no choice.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you didn't want to go, what would happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there people who refused?

speaker: interviewee
text: If-- people who refused were, at worst, caught and brought to the ghetto, or shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see someone shot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, uh huh. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were a kid.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was basically just scared. And once something like that happens, the-- one's so shocked with the-- with this incident that it's hard to imagine. And it's a lot like being in a car accident, that-- that one gets spun around, and then-- and then just only later becomes scared and gets the shakes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you see shot-- being shot?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a man who was, for some reason, escaping from a-- from a German officer. And he just pulled out his gun and shot him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever afraid that might happen to your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the time. While we were in the ghetto, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was-- my friend and I went to take a-- a woman to the-- to the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a small woman. She was an older lady. And she needed to-- she was very ill. So he and I got her in a stretcher and we took her to the hospital. And on the way back, it was curfew time. We were a little bit--

speaker: interviewer
text: What time? What time? Well,

speaker: interviewee
text: It was 9 o'clock. And we were a little bit past that time. And as we were coming around a corner, there was the guard standing there. And he was removing his rifle and getting ready to shoot. And my friend--

speaker: interviewer
text: To shoot children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. So he ran one direction. And I was slightly behind him, so I started running the other direction. And as I-- and he shot at him first and missed him, then turned and shot at me. And he missed me, because I had just managed to jam myself into a entryway in another apartment house that was sort of recessed a little bit. And so I stood there. And I thought maybe I was shot. You know, I heard that when you were shot, you can't tell or something. Anyway, I was just a kid. I knocked on the door-- only-- all reflex, of course. And somebody actually opened the door. And I spent the rest of the night there. And my parents and my sister and brothers didn't know where I was, or whether I was alive or dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in 1941. I must have been about 11 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of impression did that make on you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I never went past that spot again. I used to go make a big turn to get to where I was going, to-- to avoid that place where the guard stood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you ever seen a guard shoot a child? Was that common? Was it uncommon?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see it personally, but I saw the results.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your father and mother. What did they say when you came back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were-- they were in shock when I-- when it happened, and they were so excited. My mother mostly was to happy to see me and mad that it happened to me, you know, I mean, just two together. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So those are kind of incidences I-- we went through. And it was constant. It was-- there was always that-- life in the ghetto was-- the worst part was, for one thing, we didn't have enough food. The other was this insecurity, constant danger of being shot or getting into some kind of trouble.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you hungry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of the time. Yeah. Most of the time. Well, my father got this-- a job across the street from where he used to work. There was this factory where he did some work prior to the invasion. And when the Germans came in, of course they confiscated all these factories and he lost his job. But that factory across the street was taken over by a German officer, who came to Krakow and came to Poland, as so many did, to seek their fortunes, because it was a-- quite a lucrative thing to do for these Germans to come into a newly-occupied land. And this particular person was a German officer. He was a-- in the counterintelligence. And he served the German army and the German-- the Nazis well, so he deserved the-- to be given this factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oskar Schindler. He took over a company called Emalia, where they made pots and pans before the war. And since he heard of my father and that he didn't have a job in the-- in his own place, and so Schindler hired him. And so as a result, my father ended up to be one of the first Jews that Schindler hired.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father tell you about Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, certainly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever meet him, as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. I met Schindler quite a-- quite a few times. I ended up on Schindler's list and in Schindler's company, working. When-- when he was-- then when we were in the ghetto and my father had a job outside of the ghetto, as so many did, it was a great advantage to be able to leave the ghetto to go to work and then come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because that way we had a chance to contact non-Jewish people on the outside and able to organize and get some additional food. And then it was an advantage. My father would-- used to bring stuff stuffed in his pockets.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where would he find that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, we had the exchange, because in the same-- in the factory where he worked as a Jew, there were other working people who were non-Jews. So there was a certain amount of contact. So in that way, it was an advantage to have a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your mother? Did she have a job?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she had a job in the ghetto. She was a cleaning lady. She was cleaning barracks and offices.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did she take to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a hardworking lady. She could-- it was not hard for her to do this. She had a-- she had a hard life all along, you know, taking care of  four children, five children. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Between the two of them, was there enough money to take care of the family, to keep food on the table?

speaker: interviewee
text: Between-- between-- between the two of them and what we could do as children to earn something, we were able to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: All five of you worked, all five children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody did something. Everybody did something.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were all expected to bring money into the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a natural thing. A normal thing to do was to bring it home. And my mother would then use it to put food on the table. But I must mention this, that at the time that we were in the ghetto, we were only-- there were only four children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the-- who was--

speaker: interviewee
text: The oldest one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Hershel. He-- he didn't stay around for the Germans to overtake him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did he go?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a 19-year-old, strong young man. And he-- you know, during a wartime, when invading armies are about to, you know, invade or about to enter, the people take to the road. They try to outrun the army. They try to find a safer place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did Hershel go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hershel went east as fast as he could. And he did outrun the German army, up on the Soviet side, where his grandparents lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Narewka?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Narewka. So during that period of time, between 1939 and 1941, he was living in-- on the Soviet side. And he also spent some time in the Soviet Union, working. And then he became ill. He had an operation-- and this is what we've heard after the war-- and came back to Narewka in 1941, to his grandparents, to recuperate.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was he then?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1941, he was 21.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he met the same fate as the rest of the population [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in 1941, when the Germans invaded and the special troops murdered everyone in town, including him.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was buried in the mass grave there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he's in the common grave, in Narewka.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you go back to Narewka, you're going to him also.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yep, that's right, and to everyone else that I remember in my mind. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so we were the four of us. And my sister, my older sister, got-- also got a job that was outside of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she do?

speaker: interviewee
text: She worked in a company called Kabelwerk. That's a-- a company that did electrical-- made electrical parts, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] various kinds of cables and connections and things like that, and plugs. And it was a war-related factory, so she got a job and she was able to also get out of the ghetto and come back. But soon they created a camp around or near the company. And then she had to go and live there, near the-- in this camp. It was a labor camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you frightened for her safety? Were you concerned?

speaker: interviewee
text: Always, yeah. But the thing was, you know, at the time, these things were happening and no one had any control over this. There was no control over it. There was no-one-- there is nothing anyone could do.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as we were going along, you would-- you would think, well, why didn't just-- why didn't just people leave or why didn't they do something? Well, there was-- for one thing, we were living-- this-- this was our home. This was our place of-- and people just don't pick up and move. And there was no place to go. As we know today, we know that some people did try to go somewhere, like Switzerland and they were refused, and other places.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was the same and even worse to, say, escape from-- from the ghetto, for example. I could have-- I slipped out of the ghetto many times as a child. And I didn't look much Jewish, so people didn't pay much attention to me. But I couldn't stay out there, because I would be discovered, sooner or later, and be turned in.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the end of Tape 2. We'll continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: Already?
